xml_structure.explanation: TwinCAT XML files must be well-formed XML compliant with XML 1.0 specification. All elements must be properly opened and closed, attributes must be quoted, and special characters must be escaped.
xml_structure.why_it_matters: Malformed XML prevents TwinCAT XAE from parsing the file during import. The IDE will fail silently or show cryptic error messages, making it impossible to load the project.

correct_examples.code:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<TcPlcObject Version="1.1.0.1" ProductVersion="3.1.4024.0">
  <POU Name="FB_Example" Id="{a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890}">
  </POU>
</TcPlcObject>

common_mistakes.reason: All XML tags must have matching closing tags
Use > for > and < for < outside CDATA sections

xml_structure.twincat_reference: TwinCAT 3 XAE uses standard XML parsing; any XML parsing error causes import failure

guid_format.explanation: GUIDs (Globally Unique Identifiers) in TwinCAT must follow RFC 4122 format with lowercase hexadecimal notation: {xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx} where x is 0-9 or a-f.
guid_format.why_it_matters: Invalid GUID formats break object linking in TwinCAT IDE. The project may fail to load, or objects may appear orphaned in the solution tree without proper references.

correct_examples.code: <POU Name="FB_Motor" Id="{a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890}">
<Itf Name="I_Moveable" Id="{12345678-90ab-cdef-1234-567890abcdef}">

common_mistakes.reason: TwinCAT requires lowercase hex (a-f), not uppercase (A-F)
Placeholder is not a valid GUID format; use Tools > Generate GUID in Visual Studio
GUIDs must be wrapped in curly braces {}

guid_format.twincat_reference: TwinCAT XAE validates GUID format during XML import; non-conformant GUIDs cause silent failures

guid_uniqueness.explanation: Every POU, interface, method, property, and DUT in a TwinCAT project must have a unique GUID. Duplicate GUIDs cause object reference conflicts.
guid_uniqueness.why_it_matters: Duplicate GUIDs break the TwinCAT project database. Objects may be linked incorrectly, breakpoints may trigger in wrong locations, and project corruption can occur.

common_mistakes.reason: Each object must have a unique GUID; regenerate after copy-paste

guid_uniqueness.twincat_reference: GUID uniqueness is enforced at project level, not file level; use validate_batch for cross-file checking

indentation.explanation: TwinCAT XAE requires exactly 2-space indentation for all XML elements. Tabs and 4-space indentation are not allowed.
indentation.why_it_matters: Inconsistent indentation causes import errors or silent rejections in TwinCAT IDE. The XAE editor auto-formats with 2 spaces, so files with incorrect indentation are reformatted on first edit, causing version control churn.

correct_examples.code:
<Declaration>
  <ObjectProperties>
    <Name>MyObject</Name>
  </ObjectProperties>
</Declaration>

common_mistakes.reason: TwinCAT strictly enforces 2-space indentation, not 4
Tabs are not allowed; always use 2 spaces
Indentation must be multiples of 2 (0, 2, 4, 6, ...)

indentation.twincat_reference: XAE auto-formats on file open; indentation errors are immediately visible in diff tools

tabs.explanation: TwinCAT XML files must not contain tab characters (\t). All indentation must use space characters only, specifically 2 spaces per level.
tabs.why_it_matters: Tab characters cause TwinCAT XAE to reject the file during import or auto-format it on first edit, creating version control conflicts.

correct_examples.code:
<POU>
  <Declaration>
    <Name>FB_Test</Name>
  </Declaration>
</POU>

common_mistakes.reason: Configure your editor to use spaces, not tabs (most editors have 'insert spaces' option)
Never mix tabs and spaces; use spaces consistently

tabs.twincat_reference: TwinCAT XAE will auto-convert tabs to 2 spaces on file open

file_ending.explanation: TwinCAT XML files must end with exactly </TcPlcObject> followed by a single newline. Extra content after the closing tag (like ]]>) breaks parsing.
file_ending.why_it_matters: Extra content after </TcPlcObject> causes XML parsing errors in TwinCAT XAE. The file may appear to load but fail during compilation or cause IDE crashes.

correct_examples.code:
</POU>
</TcPlcObject>

common_mistakes.reason: CDATA closing ]]> should never appear outside CDATA sections
Use exactly one newline after </TcPlcObject>
POSIX standard requires files to end with a newline

file_ending.twincat_reference: TwinCAT XAE strictly validates XML document termination

property_var_blocks.explanation: Property getter/setter accessors in TwinCAT function blocks must declare a VAR...END_VAR block, even if empty. This is required for ST (Structured Text) compilation.
property_var_blocks.why_it_matters: Missing VAR blocks cause compilation errors: 'syntax error: missing END_VAR'. Properties without VAR blocks will fail to compile in TwinCAT XAE.

correct_examples.code:
<Get Name="Get" Id="{guid}">
  <Declaration><![CDATA[VAR
END_VAR
]]></Declaration>
</Get>

<Get Name="Get" Id="{guid}">
  <Declaration><![CDATA[VAR
  nLocal : INT;
END_VAR
]]></Declaration>
</Get>

common_mistakes.reason: Property getters must have VAR...END_VAR block
All property accessors require a Declaration element

property_var_blocks.twincat_reference: TwinCAT ST compiler requires VAR blocks for all property accessors; applies to FB and program properties

lineids_count.explanation: LineIds are metadata entries that map source code lines to their locations in the XML. The count of LineIds entries must equal the number of methods/properties plus one for the main POU body.
lineids_count.why_it_matters: Incorrect LineIds counts prevent TwinCAT debugger from setting breakpoints correctly. Breakpoints may be placed in wrong methods or not trigger at all.

correct_examples.code:
<LineIds Name="FB_Test">
  <LineId Id="2" Count="0" />
</LineIds>
<LineIds Name="FB_Test.Execute">
  <LineId Id="2" Count="0" />
</LineIds>

common_mistakes.code: // Added M_NewMethod but forgot to add LineIds entry
common_mistakes.reason: Every method/property needs a corresponding LineIds entry

common_mistakes.code: // Deleted M_OldMethod but LineIds entry still exists
common_mistakes.reason: Remove LineIds entries when deleting methods/properties

lineids_count.twincat_reference: TwinCAT XAE generates LineIds automatically; manual XML editing requires manual LineIds maintenance

element_ordering.explanation: TwinCAT XML elements must appear in specific order: Declaration before Implementation, and Methods/Properties after the main Implementation section.
element_ordering.why_it_matters: Incorrect element ordering causes TwinCAT XAE import errors. The IDE expects a specific XML schema and will reject out-of-order elements.

correct_examples.code:
<POU>
  <Declaration>...</Declaration>
  <Implementation>...</Implementation>
  <Method>...</Method>
  <Property>...</Property>
</POU>

common_mistakes.reason: Declaration must come before Implementation
Methods must come after Implementation

element_ordering.twincat_reference: TwinCAT XAE enforces strict XML schema ordering

naming_conventions.explanation: TwinCAT naming conventions use prefixes to identify object types: FB_ for function blocks, PRG_ for programs, I_ for interfaces, ST_ for structs, E_ for enums, T_ for type aliases, and GVL_ for global variable lists.
naming_conventions.why_it_matters: Consistent naming conventions improve code readability and project navigation. Many TwinCAT teams enforce naming standards via code reviews.

correct_examples.code: <POU Name="FB_MotorControl">
<Itf Name="I_Moveable">
<DUT Name="ST_Position">

common_mistakes.reason: Function blocks should start with FB_
Interfaces should start with I_

naming_conventions.twincat_reference: Naming conventions follow Beckhoff style guide; see TwinCAT documentation

cdata_formatting.explanation: CDATA sections in TwinCAT XML should not have trailing newlines before the closing ]]>. Property declarations in particular should be on a single line within CDATA.
cdata_formatting.why_it_matters: Extra newlines in CDATA cause TwinCAT XAE to auto-format files on open, creating version control noise.

correct_examples.code: <Declaration><![CDATA[PROPERTY CurrentSpeed : INT]]></Declaration>

common_mistakes.reason: No newline should appear before ]]> in property declarations

cdata_formatting.twincat_reference: TwinCAT XAE auto-formats CDATA sections to remove trailing whitespace

excessive_blank_lines.explanation: TwinCAT XML files should not contain more than 2 consecutive blank lines. Excessive blank lines are considered poor formatting.
excessive_blank_lines.why_it_matters: Excessive blank lines bloat file size and reduce code density. TwinCAT XAE will reformat files to remove excess whitespace.

correct_examples.code:
</Method>

<Method Name="Next">

common_mistakes.reason: Limit to maximum 2 consecutive blank lines

excessive_blank_lines.twincat_reference: TwinCAT XAE removes excessive blank lines on auto-format

pou_structure.explanation: FUNCTION POUs in TwinCAT cannot contain Methods or Properties. Only FUNCTION_BLOCK and PROGRAM POUs support methods and properties.
pou_structure.why_it_matters: FUNCTIONs with methods/properties fail TwinCAT compilation with error: 'Functions cannot have methods'. This is a language-level restriction.

correct_examples.code:
<POU Name="F_Calculate" PouType="Function">
  <Declaration>...</Declaration>
  <Implementation>...</Implementation>
</POU>

<POU Name="FB_Controller" PouType="FunctionBlock">
  <Declaration>...</Declaration>
  <Implementation>...</Implementation>
  <Method Name="Execute">...</Method>
</POU>

common_mistakes.reason: FUNCTIONs cannot have methods; use FUNCTION_BLOCK instead

pou_structure.twincat_reference: IEC 61131-3 language specification: FUNCTIONs are stateless and cannot have methods

pou_structure_header.explanation: POU header integrity check. Catches misfiled INTERFACE declarations stored in .TcPOU files (must be .TcIO), inline STRUCT definitions inside VAR sections, and invalid EXTENDS usage (only FUNCTION_BLOCK can extend).
pou_structure_header.why_it_matters: A .TcPOU file containing INTERFACE will be silently ignored or cause import errors. Inline STRUCTs in VAR sections are not valid IEC 61131-3. EXTENDS on FUNCTION or PROGRAM causes compile errors.

correct_examples.code:
<Itf Name="I_Sensor">
  <Declaration>INTERFACE I_Sensor
END_INTERFACE
</Declaration>
</Itf>

common_mistakes.reason: Interfaces must be stored in .TcIO files with an <Itf> root element

pou_structure_header.twincat_reference: TwinCAT file type conventions: .TcIO for interfaces, .TcPOU for function blocks/functions/programs

pou_structure_methods.explanation: Method-level structural integrity check. Detects METHOD declarations inside the main <Implementation><ST> block, VAR blocks inside method implementations, invalid RETURN syntax, mutations to VAR_INPUT parameters, and assignments to undeclared symbols.
pou_structure_methods.why_it_matters: TwinCAT methods must be separate <Method> XML elements, not inlined into the main body. VAR blocks in method implementations cause parse errors. Mutating VAR_INPUT violates IEC 61131-3 input semantics.

correct_examples.code:
<Method Name="M_Run">
  <Declaration>METHOD M_Run : BOOL
END_VAR
</Declaration>
  <Implementation><ST>M_Run := TRUE;
</ST></Implementation>
</Method>

common_mistakes.mistake: METHOD keyword inside main Implementation ST block
common_mistakes.reason: Methods must be separate XML elements, not inline in the main body

pou_structure_methods.twincat_reference: TwinCAT XML file format: methods are child XML elements of the POU, not inline ST code

main_var_input_mutation.explanation: Detects assignments to POU-level VAR_INPUT symbols inside the main <Implementation><ST> block.
main_var_input_mutation.why_it_matters: VAR_INPUT represents caller-owned inputs and should be treated as read-only. Writing to input pins creates hidden state and non-deterministic behavior across scan cycles.

correct_examples.description: Use internal latch instead of mutating input

common_mistakes.mistake: Mutating a POU VAR_INPUT in main body
common_mistakes.reason: This writes to caller-provided input instead of using internal state

main_var_input_mutation.twincat_reference: IEC 61131-3 input semantics: VAR_INPUT values are inputs to execution and should not be reassigned

pou_structure_interface.explanation: IMPLEMENTS clause contract check. Verifies that interface files (.TcIO) exist for each interface listed in IMPLEMENTS, and that method signatures match the interface declaration.
pou_structure_interface.why_it_matters: An IMPLEMENTS clause without a matching .TcIO file causes import failure. Signature mismatches between implementation and interface cause compile errors.

correct_examples.code:
FUNCTION_BLOCK FB_Controller IMPLEMENTS I_Controller
VAR
END_VAR

common_mistakes.mistake: IMPLEMENTS clause referencing a missing .TcIO file
common_mistakes.reason: Create I_Missing.TcIO with the interface declaration

pou_structure_interface.twincat_reference: TwinCAT OOP: IMPLEMENTS requires matching .TcIO interface file in the same project

pou_structure_syntax.explanation: Basic ST syntax guard for the main implementation block. Checks for unbalanced control flow keywords (IF/END_IF, FOR/END_FOR, WHILE/END_WHILE, CASE/END_CASE) and missing semicolons after assignments/control statements.
pou_structure_syntax.why_it_matters: Unbalanced keywords or missing statement terminators can cause TwinCAT parser errors and prevent compilation. Early detection avoids costly debugging.

common_mistakes.reason: ST requires semicolons to terminate statements

pou_structure_syntax.twincat_reference: IEC 61131-3 ST syntax: assignments/control statements require terminators; control keywords must be balanced

unsigned_loop_underflow.explanation: Detects FOR-loop upper bounds of the form 'unsigned_symbol - 1' where unsigned_symbol is USINT/UINT/UDINT/ULINT.
unsigned_loop_underflow.why_it_matters: If the unsigned symbol is zero, subtracting 1 wraps to max value, causing massive loop ranges and possible out-of-bounds access.

common_mistakes.mistake: Unsigned underflow in FOR upper bound
common_mistakes.reason: nCount is UINT, so 0 - 1 underflows to 65535

unsigned_loop_underflow.twincat_reference: IEC 61131-3 integer arithmetic semantics with unsigned types and TwinCAT FOR-loop bounds

pou_structure_subtype.explanation: POU subtype constraint check for FUNCTION and PROGRAM. FUNCTIONs must have a return type and cannot have Methods, Properties, or Actions. PROGRAMs should not have Properties.
pou_structure_subtype.why_it_matters: FUNCTIONs without return types fail to compile. FUNCTIONs with methods/properties/actions violate IEC 61131-3 language semantics and cause TwinCAT compile errors.

correct_examples.code:
FUNCTION F_Calculate : INT
VAR_INPUT
  nA : INT;
END_VAR

common_mistakes.code:
FUNCTION F_Calculate
VAR_INPUT
  nA : INT;
END_VAR
common_mistakes.reason: Add return type: FUNCTION F_Calculate : INT

pou_structure_subtype.twincat_reference: IEC 61131-3: FUNCTION must have a return type; FUNCTIONs are stateless and cannot have methods or properties

pou_structure_var_protected.explanation: SERVER CONTRACT: 'VAR PROTECTED' and 'VAR_PROTECTED' are forbidden variable block keywords in this project. TwinCAT IEC 61131-3 does not support a 'VAR PROTECTED' section. Encapsulation must be expressed through METHOD or PROPERTY access specifiers (e.g. METHOD PROTECTED). Using plain VAR...END_VAR for internal state is the correct pattern.
pou_structure_var_protected.why_it_matters: VAR PROTECTED is not a valid TwinCAT 3 variable block keyword — it is a hallucination. TwinCAT will refuse to import or compile any POU containing this keyword. The correct encapsulation mechanism is to declare variables in VAR...END_VAR and expose them through protected methods or properties.

correct_examples.description: Correct: internal state in VAR block, exposed via PROTECTED method
correct_examples.code:
FUNCTION_BLOCK FB_Example
VAR
  _nValue : INT;  (* internal state *)
END_VAR

// In a Method XML element:
METHOD PROTECTED GetValue : INT
VAR_INPUT
END_VAR
GetValue := _nValue;

correct_examples.description: Correct: PROTECTED property exposing internal state

common_mistakes.mistake: Using VAR PROTECTED block (space variant — forbidden)
common_mistakes.code:
FUNCTION_BLOCK FB_Example
VAR PROTECTED
  nValue : INT;
END_VAR
common_mistakes.reason: VAR PROTECTED is not a valid TwinCAT keyword. Use VAR...END_VAR with protected METHOD/PROPERTY access specifiers instead.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using VAR_PROTECTED block (underscore variant — equally forbidden)
common_mistakes.code:
FUNCTION_BLOCK FB_Example
VAR_PROTECTED
  nValue : INT;
END_VAR
common_mistakes.reason: VAR_PROTECTED is not a valid TwinCAT keyword. Use VAR...END_VAR with protected METHOD/PROPERTY access specifiers instead.

pou_structure_var_protected.twincat_reference: TwinCAT 3 OOP: valid VAR section qualifiers are VAR, VAR_INPUT, VAR_OUTPUT, VAR_IN_OUT, VAR_GLOBAL, VAR CONSTANT, VAR RETAIN, VAR PERSISTENT. PROTECTED visibility is an access modifier on METHOD/PROPERTY, not a VAR section.

extends_visibility.explanation: When a FUNCTION_BLOCK extends a base FUNCTION_BLOCK, derived code must not access base variables directly unless exposed through the base API.
extends_visibility.why_it_matters: Accessing private base state from derived classes breaks encapsulation and can fail compile depending on TwinCAT visibility rules.

correct_examples.description: Derived class uses protected base API

common_mistakes.mistake: Derived class writes base private field
common_mistakes.reason: Base private fields are not part of derived API surface

extends_visibility.twincat_reference: TwinCAT OOP visibility semantics: expose inherited state through protected methods/properties

override_marker.explanation: Overriding methods should be explicitly marked with {attribute 'override'} in TwinCAT declarations.
override_marker.why_it_matters: Explicit override markers prevent accidental shadowing and make inheritance contracts deterministic for static validation.

correct_examples.code:
{attribute 'override'}
METHOD M_Start : BOOL

common_mistakes.reason: Unmarked shadowing can hide intent and produce fragile inheritance behavior

override_marker.twincat_reference: TwinCAT method attributes documentation for override semantics

override_signature.explanation: Overridden methods must keep the same return type and ordered parameter signature as the base declaration.
override_signature.why_it_matters: Signature drift in derived methods breaks polymorphism and may fail compile or silently break runtime dispatch assumptions.

correct_examples.code:
Base: METHOD M_Set : BOOL VAR_INPUT nTarget : INT; END_VAR
Derived: METHOD M_Set : BOOL VAR_INPUT nTarget : INT; END_VAR

common_mistakes.reason: Override signatures must be exact-compatible

override_signature.twincat_reference: IEC 61131-3 OOP method override compatibility requirements

interface_contract.explanation: A POU implementing an interface must implement all interface methods/properties with matching signatures and accessor mutability.
interface_contract.why_it_matters: Missing or mismatched members against .TcIO contracts lead to non-compilable or behaviorally inconsistent component interfaces.

correct_examples.code:
INTERFACE I_Log PROPERTY P_Level : INT (Get/Set)
FUNCTION_BLOCK FB_Logger IMPLEMENTS I_Log PROPERTY P_Level : INT (Get/Set)

common_mistakes.code: Interface has Set accessor, implementation only has Get
common_mistakes.reason: Accessor contract is part of interface definition and must match

interface_contract.twincat_reference: TwinCAT interface implementation contract for methods and properties

policy_interface_contract_integrity.explanation: When policy enforcement is strict, abstract base classes that clearly match one or more interface contracts must keep explicit IMPLEMENTS declarations when they serve active derived classes. Abstract methods are valid in the base; concrete descendants can satisfy the unresolved members through inheritance-aware resolution.
policy_interface_contract_integrity.why_it_matters: Removing IMPLEMENTS from abstract bases can hide contract intent and bypass interface validation workflows while still appearing structurally valid in derived classes. It can also trigger anti-pattern workarounds such as de-abstracting bases or inserting trivial stubs.

correct_examples.description: Abstract base keeps explicit interface contract
correct_examples.code: FUNCTION_BLOCK ABSTRACT FB_MotorBase IMPLEMENTS I_MotorControl

common_mistakes.reason: This can bypass interface contract checks in strict OOP workflows when descendants rely on inherited behavior

common_mistakes.code: FUNCTION_BLOCK FB_Derived EXTENDS FB_MotorBase IMPLEMENTS I_MotorControl
common_mistakes.reason: If base already declares IMPLEMENTS, re-declaring in derived class is usually redundant and can drift from inherited contract intent

policy_interface_contract_integrity.twincat_reference: Project policy contract for fail-closed OOP validation in generated/imported TwinCAT XML

extends_cycle.explanation: EXTENDS hierarchies must be acyclic. A FUNCTION_BLOCK cannot (directly or indirectly) inherit from itself.
extends_cycle.why_it_matters: Cyclic inheritance is an invalid OOP graph and leads to compile-time model resolution failure.

correct_examples.code: FB_Advanced EXTENDS FB_Base; FB_Base EXTENDS FB_Core; FB_Core has no EXTENDS

common_mistakes.code: FB_A EXTENDS FB_B, FB_B EXTENDS FB_A
common_mistakes.reason: Cycles in base chain are invalid

extends_cycle.twincat_reference: TwinCAT OOP type system requires an acyclic EXTENDS graph

override_super_call.explanation: Certain lifecycle overrides should invoke SUPER^ to preserve required base side effects and invariants.
override_super_call.why_it_matters: Skipping base call in lifecycle methods can silently break base state machine transitions, counters, and safety guards.

correct_examples.description: Lifecycle override that preserves base behavior

common_mistakes.reason: Base stop logic may be skipped unintentionally

override_super_call.twincat_reference: TwinCAT inheritance best-practice: call SUPER^ for lifecycle overrides unless explicitly designed otherwise

inheritance_property_contract.explanation: If a derived FUNCTION_BLOCK redeclares a property from its base chain, it must preserve property type and accessor mutability (Get/Set).
inheritance_property_contract.why_it_matters: Changing property mutability or type in derived classes breaks substitutability and causes compile/API contract inconsistency.

correct_examples.code: Base PROPERTY P_Mode : INT with Get/Set; Derived PROPERTY P_Mode : INT with Get/Set

common_mistakes.code: Base has Get/Set, Derived only Get
common_mistakes.reason: Mutability contract must remain stable across inheritance

inheritance_property_contract.twincat_reference: TwinCAT OOP contract compatibility for inherited members

fb_init_signature.explanation: FB_init in TwinCAT FUNCTION_BLOCKs should keep canonical signature with BOOL return and leading VAR_INPUT parameters bInitRetains and bInCopyCode.
fb_init_signature.why_it_matters: Incorrect FB_init signature can break initialization lifecycle integration and produce non-portable behavior across projects.

common_mistakes.code:
METHOD FB_init : BOOL
VAR_INPUT
  nMode : INT;
END_VAR
common_mistakes.reason: bInitRetains and bInCopyCode should be the first required inputs

fb_init_signature.twincat_reference: TwinCAT lifecycle method FB_init signature conventions

fb_init_super_call.explanation: When a derived block overrides FB_init and base FB_init exists, it should call SUPER^.FB_init(...) to preserve base initialization.
fb_init_super_call.why_it_matters: Skipping base FB_init risks partially initialized base state and hard-to-debug startup behavior.

common_mistakes.mistake: No base init call in override
common_mistakes.reason: Base initialization path is bypassed

fb_init_super_call.twincat_reference: TwinCAT inheritance lifecycle best-practice for FB_init

this_pointer_consistency.explanation: If a method declares a local symbol with the same name as a class member, member writes should use THIS^ to avoid ambiguity.
this_pointer_consistency.why_it_matters: Name shadowing can silently write local variables instead of class state, causing subtle logic faults.

correct_examples.description: Explicit member write with THIS^ under shadowing

common_mistakes.reason: May update local symbol instead of class member

this_pointer_consistency.twincat_reference: TwinCAT ST pointer/member access conventions for THIS^

abstract_contract.explanation: ABSTRACT methods should have declaration-only behavior, and concrete derived classes must implement all inherited abstract methods.
abstract_contract.why_it_matters: Violating abstract contracts causes inheritance inconsistencies and compile/runtime contract drift in TwinCAT OOP hierarchies.

correct_examples.description: Concrete class implements inherited abstract method
Abstract class with abstract method declaration-only
correct_examples.code:
FUNCTION_BLOCK ABSTRACT FB_Base
METHOD ABSTRACT M_Run : BOOL

common_mistakes.code:
METHOD ABSTRACT M_Run : BOOL
M_Run := TRUE;
common_mistakes.reason: Abstract methods should not define executable implementation

common_mistakes.mistake: Concrete class leaves inherited abstract method unimplemented
common_mistakes.reason: Concrete classes must implement all abstract methods from base chain

abstract_contract.twincat_reference: TwinCAT OOP abstract inheritance contract rules

fb_exit_contract.explanation: FB_exit is the TwinCAT destructor method with a canonical signature: METHOD FB_exit : BOOL with VAR_INPUT (bInCopyCode : BOOL). When a FUNCTION_BLOCK uses __NEW() for dynamic allocation, it MUST have an FB_exit method to clean up resources.
fb_exit_contract.why_it_matters: Missing or malformed FB_exit prevents proper cleanup of dynamically allocated objects, causing memory leaks in PLC runtime. Memory leaks accumulate across PLC cycles and can crash the runtime or cause unpredictable behavior.

common_mistakes.reason: FB_exit MUST return BOOL (always return TRUE for success)

common_mistakes.code:
METHOD FB_exit : BOOL
VAR_INPUT
  nMode : INT;
END_VAR
common_mistakes.reason: FB_exit parameter must be named bInCopyCode and typed as BOOL
Dynamic allocation REQUIRES cleanup in FB_exit to prevent memory leaks

fb_exit_contract.twincat_reference: FB_exit is called by TwinCAT runtime before instance destruction; bInCopyCode=TRUE means online change, FALSE means actual destruction

dynamic_creation_attribute.explanation: When a FUNCTION_BLOCK is instantiated dynamically via __NEW(TypeName), the target FB MUST have {attribute 'enable_dynamic_creation'} in its declaration. Without this attribute, __NEW() returns 0 (NULL pointer) silently at runtime.
dynamic_creation_attribute.why_it_matters: Missing enable_dynamic_creation attribute causes __NEW() to silently fail without compiler warning or runtime error. The code appears correct but allocation never succeeds, leading to NULL pointer dereferences and crashes.

common_mistakes.reason: Target FB MUST have enable_dynamic_creation or allocation fails silently
Attribute must be quoted: {attribute 'enable_dynamic_creation'}

dynamic_creation_attribute.twincat_reference: TwinCAT 3 runtime requires explicit opt-in for dynamic allocation for safety; compiler does not validate this at build time
dynamic_creation_attribute.fix_suggestion: Manually add {attribute 'enable_dynamic_creation'} to the declaration of FUNCTION_BLOCK TypeName. This cannot be auto-fixed because the issue is reported in the allocator file, but the fix must be applied to the target FB file.

pointer_delete_pairing.explanation: Every pointer allocated with __NEW(Type) MUST have a matching __DELETE(pointer) in a cleanup method (FB_exit, Dispose, or custom cleanup). Failure to delete causes memory leaks in PLC runtime.
pointer_delete_pairing.why_it_matters: PLC memory is limited and not garbage-collected. Each missed __DELETE() permanently leaks memory. Over thousands of PLC cycles, this accumulates until runtime crashes or stops responding.

correct_examples.description: Allocation paired with deletion in FB_exit

common_mistakes.reason: Every __NEW() MUST have __DELETE() in cleanup path
Delete the SAME pointer variable that was allocated
Always check IF pointer <> 0 THEN before __DELETE()

pointer_delete_pairing.twincat_reference: TwinCAT PLC runtime does not have automatic garbage collection; manual memory management is mandatory

composition_depth.explanation: Deep inheritance hierarchies (>4 levels) violate SOLID principles, specifically favoring composition over inheritance. Excessive depth increases coupling, makes code harder to understand, and can lead to fragile base class problems.
composition_depth.why_it_matters: Deep inheritance chains in PLC code are harder to debug, test, and maintain. When a base class changes, all derived classes are affected. Composition (HAS-A) is more flexible than inheritance (IS-A) for TwinCAT projects.

correct_examples.description: Use composition instead of deep inheritance
correct_examples.code:
FUNCTION_BLOCK FB_Motor
VAR
  _logger : FB_Logger;  // Composition
  _validator : FB_Validator;
END_VAR

common_mistakes.reason: 5 levels of inheritance makes the hierarchy brittle and hard to maintain

composition_depth.twincat_reference: TwinCAT supports deep inheritance but SOLID principles recommend shallow hierarchies

interface_segregation.explanation: The Interface Segregation Principle states that clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use. Large interfaces (>7 members) violate this by creating fat interfaces that impose unnecessary dependencies.
interface_segregation.why_it_matters: Large interfaces make implementation classes carry bloat. Changes to one method affect all implementers. Testing becomes harder as mocks must implement all methods. PLC scan time increases with unused method overhead.

correct_examples.description: Split large interface into focused interfaces
correct_examples.code:
INTERFACE I_Readable
METHOD Read : BOOL
END_INTERFACE

INTERFACE I_Writable
METHOD Write : BOOL
END_INTERFACE

common_mistakes.code:
INTERFACE I_Device
METHOD Read, Write, Open, Close, Start, Stop, Reset, Configure, Validate, Log
END_INTERFACE
common_mistakes.reason: 10 methods force all implementers to provide all functionality, even if only 2 methods are actually needed

interface_segregation.twincat_reference: TwinCAT supports multiple interface implementation - use several small interfaces instead of one large interface

method_visibility_consistency.explanation: Liskov Substitution Principle requires derived classes to be substitutable for base classes. Reducing method visibility (PUBLIC -> PROTECTED -> PRIVATE) breaks this by making derived instances less capable than base instances.
method_visibility_consistency.why_it_matters: Reducing visibility breaks polymorphism. Code that works with base class references may fail with derived instances. This violates the contract established by the base class and causes runtime errors.

common_mistakes.code:
FB_Base: METHOD PUBLIC GetStatus
FB_Derived: METHOD PRIVATE GetStatus
common_mistakes.reason: Derived class makes public method private, breaking base class contract

method_visibility_consistency.twincat_reference: TwinCAT allows visibility reduction but it violates Liskov Substitution Principle and breaks polymorphism

diamond_inheritance_warning.explanation: Detects when the same interface is implemented in multiple classes along a single inheritance chain (e.g., both base and derived implement I_Logger). This is usually redundant since derived classes automatically inherit interface contracts. True "diamond" patterns require multiple parent paths, which TwinCAT does not support (single EXTENDS only).
diamond_inheritance_warning.why_it_matters: Redundant interface implementation adds maintenance burden without benefit. If both base and derived implement the same interface, changes to the interface require updating multiple classes. This check is a heuristic for potential design issues.

correct_examples.description: Implement interface only once in hierarchy
correct_examples.code:
FB_Base IMPLEMENTS I_Logger
FB_Derived EXTENDS FB_Base  // Inherits I_Logger from base

common_mistakes.code:
FB_Base IMPLEMENTS I_Logger
FB_Derived EXTENDS FB_Base IMPLEMENTS I_Logger  // Redundant
common_mistakes.reason: I_Logger is already inherited from FB_Base, no need to re-implement

diamond_inheritance_warning.twincat_reference: TwinCAT only supports single inheritance (one EXTENDS), so true diamond patterns (multiple parent paths) cannot occur. This check detects redundant implementation as a proxy.

abstract_instantiation.explanation: Attempting to instantiate an ABSTRACT function block with __NEW(AbstractFB) causes a runtime crash in TwinCAT. Abstract FBs are incomplete and can only be used as base classes for inheritance. They cannot be instantiated directly.
abstract_instantiation.why_it_matters: Runtime crashes are difficult to debug because __NEW() fails silently - it returns 0 (NULL pointer) without any compiler warning or runtime error message. When code tries to use the NULL pointer, the PLC crashes with cryptic 'access violation' errors.

correct_examples.code:
FUNCTION_BLOCK ABSTRACT FB_BaseMotor
...
END_FUNCTION_BLOCK

FUNCTION_BLOCK FB_ConcreteMotor EXTENDS FB_BaseMotor
...
END_FUNCTION_BLOCK

// Correct: instantiate concrete class
pMotor := __NEW(FB_ConcreteMotor);

correct_examples.description: Use abstract FB as base type only

common_mistakes.code:
FUNCTION_BLOCK ABSTRACT FB_BaseMotor
...
END_FUNCTION_BLOCK

pMotor := __NEW(FB_BaseMotor);  // CRASH
common_mistakes.reason: Abstract FBs cannot be instantiated - they are incomplete base classes only

common_mistakes.code:
// Developer may not realize FB is abstract
pController := __NEW(FB_AbstractController);
common_mistakes.reason: Check naming conventions - abstract FBs often have 'Abstract' or 'Base' prefix

abstract_instantiation.twincat_reference: TwinCAT 3 runtime does not provide compile-time validation for abstract instantiation. The check must be done at static analysis time to prevent runtime crashes.
abstract_instantiation.fix_suggestion: Cannot auto-fix - requires architectural decision. Options: 1) Create a concrete derived class and instantiate that, 2) Remove ABSTRACT keyword from target FB if it should be instantiable, 3) Use factory pattern to ensure only concrete instances are created

forbidden_abstract_attribute.explanation: TwinCAT 3 does not support {attribute 'abstract'} as a pragma. The compiler rejects files containing this pattern. Abstract behavior is declared using the ABSTRACT keyword directly in the method or class header: METHOD ABSTRACT MethodName or FUNCTION_BLOCK ABSTRACT ClassName.
forbidden_abstract_attribute.why_it_matters: Using {attribute 'abstract'} produces code that appears syntactically valid in XML but is rejected at compile time with no helpful error message, causing long debugging loops. It is a common LLM hallucination from conflating {attribute 'override'} with abstract semantics.

correct_examples.code: METHOD ABSTRACT Execute : BOOL

correct_examples.description: Abstract function block using keyword syntax
correct_examples.code: FUNCTION_BLOCK ABSTRACT FB_BaseUnit

common_mistakes.mistake: Attribute pragma before METHOD (invalid hallucination pattern)
common_mistakes.reason: {attribute 'abstract'} is not a valid TwinCAT attribute and will be rejected by the compiler. Use METHOD ABSTRACT Execute instead.

forbidden_abstract_attribute.twincat_reference: TwinCAT 3 PLC IEC 61131-3 OOP: ABSTRACT keyword (TwinCAT 3.1.4022+)

hardcoded_dispatch.explanation: Calling the same method on consecutive hardcoded array indices (arr[1].M_Reset(), arr[2].M_Reset()) is a structural anti-pattern. It is brittle, unscalable, and defeats the polymorphic purpose of interface arrays. The correct pattern is a FOR loop over the array.
hardcoded_dispatch.why_it_matters: When a new unit is added the developer must find and update every hardcoded index call site. With a FOR loop, any number of units is handled automatically. Hardcoded dispatch also prevents compile-time detection of out-of-bounds access.

correct_examples.description: Loop-based polymorphic dispatch over interface array

common_mistakes.mistake: Per-index dispatch for each array element
common_mistakes.reason: Hardcoded indices must be updated manually every time the number of units changes. Use a FOR loop instead.

hardcoded_dispatch.twincat_reference: TwinCAT 3 PLC OOP polymorphism: interface arrays and FOR-loop dispatch

method_count.explanation: Function blocks with too many methods and properties violate the Single Responsibility Principle. A POU with 15+ members likely has multiple responsibilities that should be separated into focused components.
method_count.why_it_matters: Large POUs are harder to test, maintain, and extend. They create tight coupling between unrelated behaviors, making changes risky and time-consuming. In TwinCAT, bloated FBs also increase scan cycle time and memory footprint.

correct_examples.description: Focused FB with clear single responsibility
correct_examples.code:
FUNCTION_BLOCK FB_MotorController
VAR
  _motor : I_Motor;
END_VAR

METHOD M_Start
METHOD M_Stop
METHOD M_SetSpeed
PROPERTY IsRunning
PROPERTY CurrentSpeed

common_mistakes.code:
FUNCTION_BLOCK FB_MachineController
// 25 methods handling motors, sensors, alarms, HMI, logging...
common_mistakes.reason: Combines unrelated responsibilities. Split into FB_MotorControl, FB_SensorManager, FB_AlarmHandler, etc.

method_count.twincat_reference: TwinCAT has no built-in limit on methods per POU, but SOLID design principles recommend keeping FBs focused. Default limit: 15 members (configurable via max_methods_per_pou).
method_count.fix_suggestion: Cannot auto-fix - requires design decision. Extract cohesive groups of methods into separate function blocks. Use composition (HAS-A) to combine them.

property_accessor_pairing.explanation: Properties with only a getter (read-only) or only a setter (write-only) are suspicious and often indicate incomplete implementation. While read-only properties are common and valid, write-only properties are almost always design errors.
property_accessor_pairing.why_it_matters: Write-only properties cannot be tested or monitored at runtime. Read-only properties without setters may indicate missing initialization logic. Asymmetric properties often signal incomplete refactoring or copy-paste errors.

correct_examples.description: Read-only property with getter only (valid pattern)
correct_examples.code:
PROPERTY IsRunning : BOOL
VAR
END_VAR

IsRunning := _state = E_State.Running;

common_mistakes.reason: Cannot read back the value - impossible to verify or test. Use SET accessor on normal property instead

common_mistakes.code:
PROPERTY Temperature : REAL
// Getter exists in base class, setter never added
common_mistakes.reason: Developer may have intended to add setter but forgot - asymmetric properties often indicate incomplete work

property_accessor_pairing.twincat_reference: TwinCAT allows asymmetric properties, but write-only properties violate observability principle. Read-only properties are acceptable for calculated/derived values.
property_accessor_pairing.fix_suggestion: Cannot auto-fix - requires design decision. For write-only properties: 1) Add getter to make property read-write, or 2) Convert to method (M_SetSpeed). For read-only properties: validate this is intentional (calculated property) or add setter if state should be mutable

tabs.explanation: Replaces all tab characters (\t) with 2 space characters. This ensures compliance with TwinCAT XML formatting requirements.
tabs.algorithm: Simple string replacement: content.replace('\t', '  '). Scans entire file and replaces every tab with two spaces.
tabs.risk_assessment: Safe - no data loss, purely formatting change. Reversible by manual re-tabbing (not recommended).

guid_case.explanation: Converts all GUID hex characters to lowercase. TwinCAT requires lowercase a-f, not uppercase A-F.
guid_case.algorithm: Regex pattern match on GUID format, apply .lower() to matched groups. Preserves braces and dashes.
guid_case.risk_assessment: Safe - GUIDs are case-insensitive in RFC 4122, but TwinCAT requires lowercase.

file_ending.explanation: Removes extra content after </TcPlcObject> and ensures file ends with exactly one newline.
file_ending.algorithm:
1. Remove ]]> after </TcPlcObject> using regex
2. Find last </TcPlcObject> position
3. Truncate file to </TcPlcObject> + single newline
file_ending.risk_assessment: Safe - removes invalid trailing content. Backup recommended if file has unusual structure.

newlines.explanation: Removes trailing newlines from property declarations inside CDATA sections. Property declarations should be on a single line.
newlines.risk_assessment: Safe - formatting only, no semantic change.

cdata.explanation: Alias for 'newlines' fix - fixes CDATA formatting by removing trailing newlines.

var_blocks.explanation: Adds VAR...END_VAR blocks to property getters that have empty CDATA declarations. Required for ST compilation.
var_blocks.algorithm:
1. Find all <Get> elements with empty <Declaration><![CDATA[]]></Declaration>
2. Replace with <Declaration><![CDATA[VAR\nEND_VAR\n]]></Declaration>
var_blocks.risk_assessment: Safe - adds required boilerplate. Only applies to function blocks (skips FUNCTIONs which cannot have properties).

excessive_blanks.explanation: Reduces consecutive blank lines to maximum of 2. Improves code density and reduces file size.
excessive_blanks.algorithm:
1. Split file into lines
2. Track consecutive blank lines
3. Keep first 2 blank lines, discard rest
4. Rejoin lines
excessive_blanks.risk_assessment: Safe - formatting only, no semantic change.

indentation.explanation: Fixes indentation to ensure all lines use multiples of 2 spaces. Rounds odd-numbered indentation to nearest multiple of 2.
indentation.algorithm:
1. For each line, count leading spaces
2. If not multiple of 2, round to (spaces + 1) // 2 * 2
3. Replace line with corrected indentation
indentation.risk_assessment: Medium risk - may misalign manually-formatted content. Review changes before committing.

lineids.explanation: Generates missing LineIds entries for all methods, properties, and the main POU body. Uses sequential LineId numbering.
lineids.algorithm:
1. Parse XML to find all code sections (POU body, methods, property accessors)
2. Check which sections are missing LineIds
3. Generate LineId entries: empty code gets Count=0, N lines gets Count=N-1
4. Insert before closing </POU> tag
5. Validate resulting XML
lineids.risk_assessment: Medium risk - generated LineIds may not match IDE expectations if code was manually edited. Always verify in XAE after applying. Breakpoints may need to be reset.

before_after.after:
<POU Name="FB_Test">
  <Method Name="Execute" />
  <LineIds Name="FB_Test">
    <LineId Id="2" Count="0" />
  </LineIds>
  <LineIds Name="FB_Test.Execute">
    <LineId Id="2" Count="0" />
  </LineIds>
</POU>

override_attribute.explanation: Replaces non-canonical METHOD OVERRIDE keyword syntax with TwinCAT's canonical {attribute 'override'} syntax. Ensures consistent override semantics across all derived methods.
override_attribute.algorithm:
1. Regex pattern matches METHOD\s+OVERRIDE\s+MethodName\s*:\s*ReturnType
2. Replace with {attribute 'override'}\nMETHOD MethodName : ReturnType
3. Preserves indentation and return type
override_attribute.risk_assessment: Safe - only affects method declaration syntax, not implementation logic. Purely canonical formatting change.

guid_rules.title: GUID Format & Uniqueness in TwinCAT
guid_rules.content: Every POU, interface, DUT, method, and property in TwinCAT requires a unique GUID (Globally Unique Identifier). GUIDs enable the TwinCAT IDE to track objects across renames and moves. Format: {xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx} where x is 0-9 or a-f (lowercase). TwinCAT uses GUIDs for object linking in the project database. Duplicate or invalid GUIDs cause silent failures during project load or compilation.
learning_objectives: Why GUIDs are critical for TwinCAT project integrity
How to generate valid RFC 4122 compliant GUIDs
Consequences of duplicate GUIDs across project files
How TwinCAT IDE handles GUID conflicts
Best practices: regenerate GUIDs after copy-paste operations

lineids_algorithm.title: LineIds Mapping & Code-to-XML Correlation
lineids_algorithm.content: LineIds are sequential integer markers that map source code line numbers to Declaration XML elements. They enable the TwinCAT IDE to place breakpoints correctly and correlate compiled code with source. Format: <LineIds Name="FB.Method"><LineId Id="N" Count="M" /></LineIds> where N is the starting line ID and M is the count of subsequent lines. Empty methods use Count=0. The LineId ID typically starts at 2 or 3 (1 is reserved). LineIds must be regenerated after adding/removing methods or properties.
learning_objectives: What LineIds represent in TwinCAT XML
How the IDE uses LineIds for debugging and breakpoints
Why LineId count must match method/property count + 1
How to safely regenerate LineIds using auto-fix
Risks of incorrect LineIds: misplaced breakpoints and debug confusion

xml_structure.title: TwinCAT XML Schema & Document Structure
xml_structure.content: TwinCAT uses XML 1.0 encoding=utf-8 for all project files. The root element is always <TcPlcObject> with version attributes. POU files contain: <POU> for function blocks/programs, <DUT> for data types, <GVL> for global variable lists, <Itf> for interfaces. Each file has: Declaration (variables), Implementation (code), Methods/Properties (optional), and LineIds (debugging metadata). The XML schema is strictly validated during import.
learning_objectives: Understanding TwinCAT XML file structure
Difference between .TcPOU, .TcDUT, .TcGVL, .TcIO files
Required vs optional XML elements
How TwinCAT XAE parses and validates XML
Common XML parsing errors and how to debug them

indentation_rules.title: TwinCAT XML Indentation & Formatting Standards
indentation_rules.content: TwinCAT XAE enforces 2-space indentation for all XML elements. Tabs are not allowed. Each nested element adds 2 spaces. The IDE auto-formats files on open, so non-conformant files are immediately reformatted, causing version control conflicts. Best practice: configure your editor to use 2 spaces per indent level and disable tab insertion.
learning_objectives: Why TwinCAT requires exactly 2-space indentation
How to configure VS Code / other editors for TwinCAT
Impact of incorrect indentation on version control
TwinCAT XAE auto-formatting behavior
How to batch-fix indentation across multiple files

property_syntax.title: TwinCAT Property Syntax & VAR Block Requirements
property_syntax.content: Properties in TwinCAT function blocks provide controlled access to internal state. Properties have Get and/or Set accessors. Each accessor MUST have a VAR...END_VAR block in its Declaration, even if empty. This is required for ST compilation. Properties are declared with PROPERTY keyword in the main POU Declaration, then defined separately with <Property> XML elements containing <Get> and <Set> children.
learning_objectives: Why property accessors require VAR blocks
Syntax for read-only vs read-write properties
How properties differ from methods in TwinCAT
Common compilation errors with properties
When to use properties vs public variables
